description: Eleven verses to the rising sun, drawn from Puranic compilation, carry a striking promise: daily recitation is said to keep poverty away across thousands of lifetimes. The Aditya Stotram is more than a morning praise. Tradition reaches for it to clear all diseases, with heart ailments and jaundice named specifically, and to wash away sins so the reciter may enter Surya Loka. Folded into it is the Surya Gayatri mantra for daily meditation. Sundays are the natural day to chant it, and devotees give it special weight at Makar Sankranti and Ratha Saptami. The whole thing runs about six minutes. Here you will find the full Sanskrit in Devanagari, a transliteration to guide pronunciation, the meaning rendered in Hindi, and a PDF you can download and recite away from the screen.

Beauty, victory, fame. The Argala Stotram asks Durga for all three in plain words, rupam, jayam, yasho, and that directness is part of why it sits beside the Durga Saptashati in the Markandeya Purana, where it comes from. Reciters turn to its twenty-four verses to clear away enemies and obstacles, and to draw down good fortune, health, and a happiness the text calls supreme. Knowledge and wealth are part of the bargain too. Tuesdays and Fridays suit it best, and during Navratri it becomes almost unavoidable. Reading it through takes roughly ten minutes. This page sets out the complete hymn in Devanagari, adds an English transliteration for those who do not read the script, gives the Hindi meaning verse by verse, and offers a free PDF for offline chanting.

Twelve names, one lord who is son to both Vishnu and Shiva. The Ayyappa Dwadash Nama Stotram gathers a dozen sacred names of Lord Ayyappa into five short verses, and through the Hariharaputra epithet it calls down the combined grace of those two great deities. Pilgrims lean on it for the success of the Sabarimala vow, and devotees recite it for the complete protection it is believed to throw around them. Saturdays and Mondays are the favoured days, with Makar Sankranti adding its own weight to the chant. Three minutes is all it takes. Below you will find the verses in Devanagari, an ASCII transliteration to keep the pronunciation steady, the meaning explained in Hindi, and a downloadable PDF for recitation on the go.

Karmanye Vadhikaraste, the most quoted line in all of Hindu thought, lives in this chapter. Sankhya Yoga is the second chapter of the Bhagavad Gita within the Mahabharata, seventy-two verses in which Krishna turns Arjuna's grief into a teaching on the immortality of the soul. It lays out Nishkama Karma, action without grasping at the fruit, and sketches the Sthitaprajna, the person whose wisdom holds steady. Reciters use it to loosen the fear of death and the body's hold on them, and to build the evenness that meets success and failure alike. Wednesdays suit the reading, and Janmashtami most of all. Set aside about thirty minutes. The page carries the full chapter in Devanagari, a transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a PDF to download.

Rooted in the Rudra Yamala Tantra, the Bhairava Stotram is a hymn for those who need a hard shield. Its eleven verses are invoked against enemies, evil spirits, and black magic, and when chanted during Rahu Kaal they are said to undo that period's ill effects. Astrology-minded reciters reach for it to ease graha-dosha and other planetary afflictions, while tantric practitioners value it for the siddhis and spiritual accomplishments it can open. Tuesdays and Saturdays are its days, and it carries extra force on Kalashtami, Maha Shivaratri, and Pradosh. A reading runs about ten minutes. On this page the complete text appears in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the meaning given in Hindi, and a free PDF for recitation away from the device.

Among the Navagraha hymns, the Budha Stotram is the short prayer for Mercury, five verses carried down through the Purana tradition. Devotees chant it to invoke Budha's blessings in all his forms, and to draw the divine protection and auspiciousness the planet is believed to confer. The tradition also recites it to clear away sins and earn spiritual merit, treating it as a steady aid to devotion and inner growth rather than a one-off remedy. Wednesday is its day, the weekday Mercury rules, and Budh Purnima marks its high point in the year. Three minutes covers a full reading. Here you can study the verses in Devanagari, follow the ASCII transliteration, read the meaning in Hindi, and download a PDF to recite at your own pace.

Shankaracharya put the heart of Advaita Vedanta into ten verses, and called the teacher Dakshinamurthy, the silent guru facing south. This stotram is that teaching in praise form. Through patient philosophical inquiry it points to the true nature of the Atman, kindles jnana, and dissolves the illusion of maya. Its aim is the realization behind the Mahavakya, Tat Tvam Asi, that you are That. Mondays and Thursdays are the days devotees choose, and it gains weight on Maha Shivaratri and Guru Purnima. A full reading takes about ten minutes. This page gives the complete Sanskrit in Devanagari, an English transliteration to steady the pronunciation, the meaning unfolded in Hindi, and a PDF you can keep for offline study and recitation.

Brahma, Vishnu, and Mahesh in a single form, that is Dattatreya, and these eight verses of mangala are sung to bring his auspiciousness near. The Dattatreya Mangalashtakam comes from the Dattatreya Tantra. Reciters draw on it for the blessings of that combined trinity, for spiritual wisdom and yogic attainment, and the tradition turns to it to clear sins and grant the fulfilment of desires. Thursdays and Wednesdays are its days, and Datta Jayanti and Guru Purnima are when devotees give it the most attention. The eight verses take about five minutes to chant. On this page the hymn is laid out in Devanagari, paired with an English transliteration, explained through a Hindi meaning, and made available as a free PDF for recitation offline.

Physician of the gods, risen from the churning ocean with the pot of amrita, Dhanvantari is the deity healers pray to. His Ashtakam, eight verses from the Bhagavata Purana, is recited for freedom from disease and chronic ailment, for long life and vitality, and through his grace, for healing itself. Medical practitioners hold it especially close, chanting it as they seek success in their work. The favoured days are Tuesday and Thursday, and devotees turn to it at Dhanteras and Diwali, when the connection between health and prosperity feels closest. A reading takes around eight minutes. Here the verses appear in Devanagari, with an English transliteration alongside, the meaning given in Hindi, and a downloadable PDF so the hymn can be recited away from the screen.

Recite twelve names of Durga, the tradition holds, and every wish finds its fulfilment. The Durga Dwadash Nama Stotram packs that promise into eight verses drawn from the Devi Bhagavata Purana, and among the twelve it threads the names of the Navadurga for complete divine protection. Devotees chant it to drive out the fear of death and of enemies, both said to be removed entirely, and to reach the siddhis and spiritual attainments the goddess grants. Tuesdays and Fridays are its days, with Navratri and Durga Puja its peak occasions. Five minutes covers the reading. This page presents the full stotram in Devanagari, adds an English transliteration for pronunciation, sets out the Hindi meaning, and offers a free PDF to download for offline recitation.

The sun wears twelve names across the year, and the Dwadash Aditya Stotram, eight verses from Puranic compilation, holds all of them for daily recitation. Chanted at dawn, it is said to lift away poverty, sorrow, and disease, and to grant the perfect health and longevity the sun is believed to bestow. Reciters also look to it for wealth, sharpness of mind, and victory in whatever they set their hand to. Sunday is its day, and the chant carries extra meaning during Makar Sankranti and Chhath Puja. About five minutes sees it through. On this page the twelve-name hymn is given in Devanagari, with an English transliteration to guide the tongue, the meaning explained in Hindi, and a PDF for recitation offline.

A kavacham is armour, and the Ganesha Kavacham, ten verses from the Ganesha Purana, wraps the devotee in the full divine protection of the elephant-faced lord. It guards from every direction and at every hour, day, night, and sandhya, the dusk and dawn junctions. Reciters lean on it to clear obstacles out of career, study, and personal life, and the tradition treats it as the proper prelude before any Ganesha mantra japa, the shield that lets the mantra work at full strength. Wednesday is its day, and Ganesh Chaturthi its festival. A reading takes about eight minutes. Here you will find the complete armour-prayer in Devanagari, an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning verse by verse, and a free PDF to keep for offline recitation.

When the lamp circles before Gayatri Mata, these are the words that go with the flame. The Gayatri Aarti is a traditional worship song of seven verses, drawn from the body of Gayatri bhakti literature. Devotees sing it to invoke her blessings, to sharpen wisdom, intellect, and memory, and the tradition turns to it to lift sorrow and poverty. It is also sung to bring desired wishes and aspirations to fruition. Sunday is the day most associated with it, and Gayatri Jayanti is its festival. The aarti runs about five minutes. This page carries the full text in Devanagari, sets an English transliteration beside it for those who cannot read the script, explains the meaning in Hindi, and provides a free PDF for offline singing.

Jayadeva wrote the Gita Govinda in the twelfth century, and few works in Sanskrit literature match it for devotional beauty. These ten key songs gather its high points, including the Dashavatara Stotra that praises all ten incarnations of Vishnu. The poem deepens Krishna bhakti through shringar rasa, the mood of divine love, and is bound in tradition to surrender at Krishna's feet. Devotees sing these songs on Wednesdays, and the year brings them forward at Janmashtami, Holi, and Sharad Purnima. About eight minutes carries the selection through. On this page the songs appear in Devanagari, with an English transliteration running alongside, the meaning opened up in Hindi, and a downloadable PDF so you can sing them away from the screen.

Govinda Damodara Madhaveti, the refrain returns again and again, a nama-sankirtana that carries the whole hymn on its shoulders. Bilvamangala Thakura, a great Krishna-bhakta poet, composed these twelve verses, which survive in his Krishna Karnamrita. Each one settles on a different lila, a different pastime of Krishna's childhood, and the recitation is meant to awaken prema-bhakti, the loving devotion those pastimes inspire. The favoured days are Wednesday and Thursday, and devotees chant it at Janmashtami and Holi. A reading takes roughly eight minutes. Here the complete stotram is set in Devanagari, with an English transliteration to steady the pronunciation, the Hindi meaning explained throughout, and a free PDF you can download for recitation offline.

One hundred and eight names of Hanuman, told one after another, build the strength and courage the recitation is famous for. This Ashtottara Shatanamavali belongs to the Parasara Smriti and the wider Hanuman tradition, and priests use it during Hanuman puja, archana, and abhishek, offering a flower or a touch of kumkum at each name. The tradition reaches for it to clear away fears, evil spirits, and planetary afflictions, and to cure disease and lift every kind of suffering. Tuesdays and Saturdays are its days, and Hanuman Jayanti its festival. Reading all 108 names takes about fifteen minutes. On this page they appear in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the meaning in Hindi, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

Brahma himself prescribed it for this dark age, says the Kali Santarana Upanishad of the Krishna Yajurveda, and that is where the Hare Krishna Mahamantra comes from. Three lines, sixteen names, no fixed rule or ritual attached, anyone may chant it at any hour. The tradition holds that mere chanting burns off the kalmasha, the sins of Kali Yuga, and opens all four kinds of mukti, salokya, samipya, sarupya, and sayujya. Wednesday, Thursday, Saturday, and Sunday are all suited to it, and Janmashtami and Ram Navami bring it forward. About five minutes carries the mantra through. Here you will find it in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the meaning explained in Hindi, and a PDF to download for chanting offline.

Around the lord of Puri, his sister Subhadra, and his brother Balabhadra, this kavacham draws a wall of protection. The Jagannath Kavacham comes from the Skanda Purana and the Oriya tradition, and across its eight verses it calls on the three together for complete cover, then reaches for Vishnu's dashavatara names to guard the body part by part. Reciters chant it for divine protection, for prosperity, and for spiritual liberation. Thursdays and Saturdays are its days, and Rath Yatra, when the deities ride out of the temple, is its great occasion. A reading runs about four minutes. On this page the armour-prayer is laid out in Devanagari, paired with an English transliteration, explained through a Hindi meaning, and offered as a free PDF for offline recitation.

Three verses hold the fierce, protective fire of Kali, and the Kali Gayatri, drawn from the Kalika Purana, is how devotees call it close. This is a Vedic meditation mantra in the Gayatri form, recited to clear away fear and replace it with fearlessness, and to drive off enemies and negative forces. Practitioners of tantric sadhana value it highly for the spiritual awakening it can spark. Tuesdays and Saturdays are its days, and it gathers force during Kali Puja and Navratri. Four minutes covers a full reading. Here the mantra appears in Devanagari, with an English transliteration set beside it for pronunciation, the meaning given in Hindi, and a downloadable PDF so it can be chanted away from the screen, whenever the practice calls for it.

Kamala stands tenth among the Mahavidyas, the Tantric form of Lakshmi, and though she shares the goddess of wealth's nature her worship runs its own course, distinct and deeper. This stotram from the Tantrasara, eight verses long, invokes abundance of wealth, of grains, of material prosperity, and unusually asks for both bhukti and mukti, worldly enjoyment and liberation, in the same breath. The image of elephants bathing her stands for royal sovereignty and overflowing plenty. Fridays suit her, with Navratri, Diwali, and Akshaya Tritiya marking her high days. A reading takes about five minutes. This page sets the hymn in Devanagari, adds an English transliteration, explains the meaning in Hindi, and gives a free PDF for recitation offline.

Haare Ka Sahara, the support of the defeated, that is what devotees call Khatu Shyam Baba, the deity many name the lord of Kalyug. This aarti of eight verses, gathered from traditional aarti sangrah, is sung to him as hope for the hopeless. Worshippers sing it to clear disease, sorrow, and every difficulty, and to bring the heart's deepest desires to fulfilment. Sunday is its day, and the Phalguna Mela and Shyam Ekadashi are when his devotees gather in their largest numbers. The aarti runs about five minutes. On this page you will find the full text in Devanagari, an English transliteration for those who do not read the script, the meaning rendered in Hindi, and a free PDF to download for singing offline.

Five jewels, that is what pancharatnam means, and Shankaracharya cut these five verses to declare Krishna the supreme reality. Each one catches a facet of his divine form, the whole hymn holding the complete beauty of it, and across them move Bal-Krishna the child, Gopala the cowherd, and Govardhandhari who lifted the mountain. The aim of the recitation is krishna-sayujya, union with him, and moksha. Wednesday is the day devotees choose, and Janmashtami and Holi are its festivals. Just three minutes carries all five verses through. Here you can read the Krishna Pancharatnam in Devanagari, follow an English transliteration to keep the pronunciation right, study the meaning in Hindi, and download a free PDF so the five jewels travel with you for recitation offline.

The Brahmavaivarta Purana gives this seven-verse armour to Lakshmi, and it covers the devotee from head to foot, every body part placed under the goddess of wealth's protection. Reciters chant it to invite Lakshmi to take up permanent residence in the home, so that wealth itself settles and stays. The tradition turns to it to clear away poverty and pour out abundance of wealth and grain, and when recited each morning it is said to grant whatever the heart desires. Friday is its day, the weekday of Lakshmi, and Diwali and Varalakshmi Vrata are its festivals. A reading takes about eight minutes. On this page the kavacham appears in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

Vallabhacharya saw sweetness everywhere he looked at Krishna, his lips, his walk, his flute, his very name, and he gave that vision eight verses of madhurya. The Madhurashtakam is that hymn. Reciting it deepens love and devotion for Krishna and fills the heart with a divine sweetness and joy, and the tradition reaches for it to ease mental stress and bring inner peace. Beyond comfort, it works to purify the mind and lift consciousness. Thursdays, Sundays, and Wednesdays all suit it, and Janmashtami and Ekadashi are its occasions. About five minutes carries the eight verses through. Here the hymn is set in Devanagari, with an English transliteration beside it, the meaning unfolded in Hindi, and a downloadable PDF for recitation away from the screen.

When Mars sits ill in a chart, this is the armour devotees put on. The Mangal Graha Kavacham, one of the Navagraha prayers, comes from the Graha Stotra Sangraha and is recited as a kavach against afflictions tied to Mangal, also called Kuja. Worshippers chant it to soften the effects of mangal dosha in the horoscope, to draw the planet's benefits in land, property, and real estate, and the tradition turns to it to clear away enemies and remove disease. Tuesday, the weekday Mars rules, is its day, and Mangal Jayanti its festival. Four minutes covers the eight verses. On this page the kavacham is given in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the meaning explained in Hindi, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

Students facing examinations have long turned to the Medha Suktam, and the reason is in its name, medha, the power of retention. This Vedic hymn comes from the Taittiriya Aranyaka of the Yajurveda, nine verses that invoke a sharp intellect, a strong memory, and Saraswati's blessings for learning. It works to deepen concentration and the ability to take hold of complex subjects, which is exactly what an exam season demands. Wednesdays and Thursdays are its days, and Vasant Panchami, the festival of learning, is its high point. A reading takes about eight minutes. Here the suktam appears in Devanagari, with an English transliteration to keep the Vedic pronunciation steady, the meaning given in Hindi, and a free PDF you can download for offline recitation.

Half-man, half-lion, the form Vishnu took to tear apart a tyrant, Narasimha answers in fury when his devotees are threatened. This Ashtottara Shatanamavali from the Garuda Purana lays out 108 of his names, made for archana, the worship where a flower or a pinch of kumkum is offered at each name spoken. Reciters chant it to clear away fears, diseases, and enemies, and to draw down the courage, the strength, and the lion-lord's grace, all under his powerful protection. Saturday is its day, and Narasimha Jayanti its festival. The full recitation runs about twelve minutes. On this page the names appear in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

When the ocean was churned, poison rose before the nectar, and Shiva drank it to save the worlds, holding it in his throat until it turned blue. The Shiva Purana tells the story, and the Neelakanthesha Stotram, eight verses, sings to that blue-throated lord. Because of what he swallowed, the hymn is invoked for protection from every poison and toxicity, and reciters turn to it to clear disease and grant health, and through Shiva's boundless compassion, to remove all sins. It carries the memory of his supreme sacrifice. Monday is its day, with Maha Shivaratri and Nag Panchami its festivals. Five minutes covers the reading. Here the stotram is set in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the meaning in Hindi, and a downloadable PDF.

Five deities, one altar. Shankaracharya established the Panchayatana Puja as the foundation of Smarta Dharma, setting Ganesha, Surya, Vishnu, Shiva, and the Goddess together in worship, and this stotram of ten verses carries that whole arrangement. Inside it sit some of the most beloved stutis a Hindu household knows, Vakratunda, Shantakaram, Sarva Mangala. The teaching is balance: worship all five and you reach complete spiritual accomplishment. The hymn is associated with Navratri. A reading takes about seven minutes. On this page the five-deity prayer appears in Devanagari, with an English transliteration set beside it for those who cannot read the script, the meaning opened up in Hindi, and a free PDF to download so the stotram can be recited away from the screen at home.

A child stood before his murderous father and praised the very lord his father hated, and his words became the Prahlada Stuti. It comes from the seventh canto, ninth chapter of the Shrimad Bhagavata Purana, eight verses that show pure bhakti rising above birth, caste, and every qualification. They teach that God is pleased by devotion alone, never by material worth, and they breathe fearlessness and faith into the worst adversity, while pointing the reciter toward the company of saintly devotees. Thursdays, Sundays, and Saturdays all suit it, and Narasimha Jayanti and Holi are its festivals. About eight minutes carries it through. Here the stuti is given in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

A thousand names of Radha, drawn from the Narada Pancharatra, open a path that few hymns even attempt. The Radha Sahasranama is recited for the highest reach of Krishna-bhakti, the kind that comes only through her grace, and for the divine love, the prema, it plants in the heart. The tradition holds that it clears away sins and carries the devotee toward Goloka Dham, Krishna's own abode. Its force runs strongest during Radha Ashtami and Holi. Wednesdays and Sundays are its days. The full recitation, gathered here into twenty-five verses, takes about twenty-five minutes. On this page the thousand names appear in Devanagari, with an English transliteration to steady the pronunciation, the meaning explained in Hindi, and a free PDF to download for recitation offline.

Five verses, easy to hold in memory, and the whole essence of Rama gathered inside them, that is the Rama Panchakam, a traditional Sanskrit stotra. Its brevity is the point: short enough to chant every morning before the day begins, yet carrying all the blessings of Rama devotion in compact form. The tradition recites it to clear away sins and to lead the devotee toward Rama Sayujya, union with the lord. Sunday is its day, and Ram Navami, Dussehra, and Vivah Panchami are its festivals. Three minutes covers all five verses. Here you can read the Rama Panchakam in Devanagari, follow an English transliteration to keep the pronunciation true, study the meaning in Hindi, and download a free PDF so the five verses travel with you for recitation offline.

A thousand names of Shirdi Sai Baba, set in the traditional Sahasranama format, catch what was singular about him, saint, healer, and avatar at once. This stotram from the Sai devotional tradition weaves in the places his devotees still hold dear, Dwarkamai and the Dhuni, Lendi and the Chavadi, so that the names carry the geography of his life. Reciters chant it for the fulfilment of desires, for healing, and for spiritual liberation. Thursday, the day of the guru, is its day, and Guru Purnima its festival. The recitation, gathered here into ten verses, runs about seven minutes. On this page the names appear in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the meaning given in Hindi, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

Ya Kundendu tushara hara dhavala, the famous dhyana shloka that pictures Saraswati white as jasmine and the moon, sits inside this mantra. The Saraswati Gayatri comes from the Saraswati Rahasya Upanishad, four verses that invoke vidya, buddhi, and medha, knowledge, intellect, and memory together. Reciters chant it for vak siddhi, mastery over speech and expression, and to drive off jadya, the mental dullness that clouds clear thought. Thursdays suit it, and it comes forward at Vasant Panchami, during Navratri, and across exam seasons. Four minutes covers a reading. Here the mantra is set in Devanagari, with an English transliteration beside it for those who do not read the script, the meaning explained in Hindi, and a free PDF to download for recitation offline.

The name says it plainly: sarva karya siddhi, success in every undertaking. This stotram from the Niti Shastra, nine verses, gathers seven major deities into one universal prayer for getting things done. Reciters turn to it to clear obstacles from any task, and to win through in a job, a business, a court case, an exam. The tradition makes an unusually generous claim for it, that simply hearing the stotram is enough to bring tasks to completion. Wednesdays and Thursdays are its days. A reading takes about five minutes. On this page the complete hymn appears in Devanagari, with an English transliteration set beside it for pronunciation, the meaning unfolded in Hindi, and a free PDF you can download to recite or play offline.

Saturn's gaze is heavy, and the Shani Kavacham, eight verses from the Brahma Vaivarta Purana, is the armour devotees raise against it. The hymn covers the body part by part, shielding each from the planet's malefic influence, and the tradition reaches for it to ease the afflictions of Shani and of other planets too. Its promised reach is wide: children to the childless, wealth to the poor, freedom for the sick from disease and for the imprisoned from their bondage. Saturday is its day, the weekday Saturn rules, and Shani Jayanti its festival. Five minutes covers the reading. Here the kavacham is laid out in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the meaning given in Hindi, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

Sharabha is the most ferocious form Shiva takes, an eight-legged creature part bird, part beast, and the Sharabha Stotram sings to it across nine verses drawn from the Shiva Purana and the Sharabha Upanishad. Within this single fierce form, the hymn holds the combined power of Brahma, Vishnu, and Maheshwara. Reciters chant it to clear away enemies, diseases, and planetary afflictions, and for sarva bhaya apaha, fearlessness from every danger. Saturdays and Tuesdays are its days, and Maha Shivaratri its festival. A reading runs about six minutes. On this page the stotram appears in Devanagari, with an English transliteration to keep the pronunciation steady, the meaning explained in Hindi, and a free PDF to download for recitation away from the screen.

The Shiv Kavacham builds a divine armour around the one who recites it, a wall raised by Shiva himself in his many forms. This traditional Sanskrit hymn of nine verses guards each part of the body through a different form of the lord, and throws protection across all eight directions. Reciters chant it to clear away the fear of enemies, evil spirits, and negativity, and the tradition holds out a rare reward, a merit that even the devas find hard to win. Monday is its day, the weekday of Shiva, and Maha Shivaratri and the month of Shravan are its great occasions. About six minutes carries it through. Here the kavacham is given in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

Hridayam means the heart, the innermost essence, and the Shiva Hridayam claims exactly that for the supreme Shaiva, the very core of Shiva. It descends from Vamadeva Rishi and the Atharvana Upanishad tradition, eight verses that hold the famous Karpoora Gauram dhyanam, picturing Shiva white as camphor, and a complete Anganyasa that places his six names upon the body. The hymn reveals the Shivo'ham truth, the realization I am Shiva. Monday is its day, with Maha Shivaratri and the month of Shravan its high occasions. A reading takes about seven minutes. On this page the heart-essence prayer appears in Devanagari, with an English transliteration beside it, the meaning unfolded in Hindi, and a free PDF to download for recitation offline.

Shodashi means sixteen, the count of syllables in the supreme mantra of Tripurasundari, and this stotram invokes that sixteen-syllable form of the goddess. It belongs to the Sri Vidya Tantra and the Dakshinamurti Samhita, fourteen verses that connect the reciter to Sri Chakra worship at its highest. Devotees chant it for vidya-siddhi, mastery of learning, and vak-siddhi, eloquence, and the tradition reaches for it to grant complete saubhagya and jivan-mukti, liberation attained while still alive. Fridays and Tuesdays are its days, with Navratri and Diwali its festivals. A reading runs about ten minutes. Here the stotram is set in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the meaning explained in Hindi, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

Shyama Sundara, the dark and beautiful one, Krishna as Vrindavan knew him with the flute at his lips. This stotram from the Garga Samhita, eight verses, sings to that mesmerizing form, and across them move Bal-Gopal the child, Raas-Krishna of the moonlit dance, and Dwarkadhish the king. The recitation works toward prema-bhakti, loving devotion, and vaikuntha gati, the journey to Vishnu's abode. Wednesday is its day, and Janmashtami and Holi are its festivals. Just four minutes carries the eight verses through. On this page you can read the Shyama Sundara Stotram in Devanagari, follow an English transliteration to keep the pronunciation true, study the meaning in Hindi, and download a free PDF so the hymn travels with you for recitation offline.

Soundarya Lahari means the wave of beauty, and Shankaracharya's hymn to the Goddess is among the most celebrated in all of Sanskrit. This excerpt gathers five of its verses into a shorter recitation. Devotees chant it to invoke the blessings of Lalita in her many forms, and to draw divine protection and auspiciousness near. The tradition turns to it to clear away sins and earn spiritual merit, treating it as a steady aid to devotion and inner growth. Friday is its day, with Navratri and Lalita Panchami its festivals. About four minutes covers the five verses. Here the excerpt appears in Devanagari, with an English transliteration set beside it for those who cannot read the script, the meaning given in Hindi, and a free PDF to download for recitation offline.

Eight verses of mangala for the sun, the Surya Mangalashtakam comes from the Surya Purana and carries his blessings as auspiciousness itself. Reciters chant it for health and vitality and freedom from disease, and the tradition reaches for it to clear away sins and the darkness of ignorance, the sun standing against both kinds of dark. It also works toward success, fame, and the qualities that make a leader. Sunday is its day, and Makar Sankranti, Ratha Saptami, and Chhath Puja are its festivals, three occasions when the sun draws a nation's prayers. Five minutes covers the reading. On this page the hymn appears in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the meaning explained in Hindi, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

Govinda, Govinda, the cry that fills the hills of Tirumala, and these eleven verses are built around it. The Tirupati Stotram comes from the Venkatachala Mahatmya, and tradition holds that reciting it equals the pilgrimage to the Balaji temple itself, with the very chant of Govinda Govinda granting liberation beyond doubt. Devotees turn to it for the fulfilment of desires through Venkatesha's grace, and for vishnuloka and release from the cycle of rebirth. Saturdays and Wednesdays are its days, and Vaikuntha Ekadashi and Brahmotsavam its great festivals. A reading takes about eight minutes. Here the stotram is set in Devanagari, with an English transliteration to steady the pronunciation, the meaning given in Hindi, and a free PDF to download for recitation offline.

Bhartrihari was a king who walked away from his throne, and in the fifth century he wrote the Vairagya Shatakam to set down what he learned. This selection of ten verses cultivates a deep dispassion toward worldly attachment and awakens the discrimination between what endures and what passes. The verses work to free the mind from the bondage of desire and to stir genuine renunciation and seeking. Mondays and Saturdays suit the reading, and Maha Shivaratri and Guru Purnima are its occasions. About ten minutes carries the selection through. On this page the verses appear in Devanagari, with an English transliteration beside them for those who do not read the script, the meaning unfolded in Hindi, and a free PDF to download for reflective recitation offline.

Breath is life, and the wind god holds dominion over it. The Vayu Kavacham, seven verses from the Vayu Purana, raises the divine armour of Vayu Dev around the one who recites it. The hymn strengthens prana, the life force, throughout the body, and reciters turn to it for relief from respiratory ailments and lung disease, a fitting prayer to the deity of breath. It is chanted, too, for long life and freedom from every disease. The kavacham takes about seven minutes to read. On this page the armour-prayer is laid out in Devanagari, with an English transliteration set beside it for pronunciation, the meaning explained in Hindi, and a free PDF you can download to recite away from the screen, whenever the practice calls for it.

On his bed of arrows, Bhishma gave Yudhishthira the thousand names of Vishnu, and that teaching, set in the Anushasana Parva of the Mahabharata, became the Vishnu Sahasranama. Few recitations are held in higher regard. Devotees chant it for wealth, prosperity, and success in all they undertake, for the fulfilment of desires when said with devotion, and for the blessings of Vishnu and Lakshmi together. The tradition reaches for it to clear away sins, to shield from danger and evil, and ultimately to break the cycle of birth and death. Thursdays and Sundays are its days, with Ekadashi and Vishnu Jayanti its occasions. A full recitation, gathered here into 142 verses, takes about forty-five minutes. Here it appears in Devanagari, with transliteration, Hindi meaning, and a free PDF.
